key_insight: A unified planar 3D ILSA workflow, combined with matrix splitting and Anderson acceleration, significantly speeds up hydraulic fracture simulations without materially degrading accuracy.
engineering_relevance: These acceleration strategies make planar 3D fracture models more practical for design optimization and mini-frac interpretation, where many repeated simulations are needed.
method: Unify the elastohydrodynamic solve and front-tracking loops into one iterative planar 3D ILSA scheme.
Apply matrix splitting so the dense elasticity contribution is moved to the right-hand side, leaving a sparse linear system.
Use Anderson acceleration, with an optional predictor--corrector step, to improve convergence across changing fracture geometries.
evidence_signal: Across five benchmark cases, the unified scheme averaged 2.5x speedup, and the full combined method averaged 4x with up to 11x on the sandglass case while keeping aperture error below 5%.
watch_item: The speedups are geometry-dependent, so implementation benefits may vary for other fracture shapes or solver settings.
paper_title: Acceleration methods for the planar 3D ILSA hydraulic fracturing model
